lotl.label: What's Going On With the Jays
lotl.body: The Blue Jays came to Milwaukee thinking they could steal a series. Instead, they watched the Brewers turn baseball into origami—three consecutive bunts in the seventh inning broke a tie and buried Toronto 2-1 Thursday night, capping a sweep that dropped the Jays to 7-11 and fourth in the AL East, already four games out. This isn't a slump anymore. This is a crisis wearing a uniform. The numbers don't lie: two straight losses to a Brewers team that barely scraped into contention themselves. Toronto stranded the tying run at third in the ninth—a final indignity that summed up this entire nightmare start. Garrett Mitchell scored the go-ahead run on bunts. Bunts. The Blue Jays' offense, which should be carrying this team, managed six hits across nine innings. That's not baseball; that's surrender. Aaron Ashby took the win for Milwaukee at 5-0, now leading the majors in victories. Toronto's pitching held the line—Brandon Sproat threw 6⅔ solid innings—but it doesn't matter when your lineup can't generate anything resembling momentum. The Jays head to Arizona for a three-game set starting Saturday at 8:40 PM ET, desperate for a spark before this season spirals past recovery.

the_latest.headline: Jays fell to Brewers 1–2
the_latest.dek: Full game recap and box score.

lotl.label: What's Going On With the Raptors
lotl.body: The air crackles in Toronto this morning — the Raptors, forged in a gritty 46-36 grind that locked the Atlantic's third spot, sit coiled as the No. 5 seed, one breath from playoff thunder. No one's sleeping on this crew anymore; they've ripped off 26 wins since the calendar flipped to '26, a blistering streak underscoring their W1 momentum and why the doubters picking them for a quick Cavaliers grave in five or six games might want to think twice. Young blood Collin Murray-Boyles, the rookie forward who's been a defensive menace, dissected Draymond Green's tricks yesterday, eyes locked on Cleveland's frontcourt beasts like Jarrett Allen — while Brandon Ingram sharpened his edge in Thursday's practice, the whole squad buzzing with that first-round fire. The regular season's ashes? Buried. This is playoff Raptors, unbowed at 5.0 games back in the seed race, ready to flip the script on a Cavs squad that's feasted on lesser foes. Game 1 tips Saturday at 1 p.m. — watch Murray-Boyles hound the paint, because if Toronto's dogs hunt, Cleveland's season shatters early.

lotl.label: What's Going On With the Commanders
lotl.body: The draft smoke clears in Pittsburgh next week, but Washington’s war room already hums with purpose—Adam Peters, the steady-handed GM who dragged this 5-12 outfit to third in the NFC East despite nine games back, just laid it bare. Yesterday, Peters declared it “more likely than not” they’ll stand pat at No. 7, scoffing at trade-back talk while eyeing a coup from the board’s riches. Their six-pick arsenal—slim as a rookie contract—screams for edge rushers like Arvell Reese or David Bailey to slip down, linebackers such as Sonny Styles to ignite the second level, or corners Mansoor Delane and Jermod McCoy to lock the secondary, positions Peters hosted at Top Golf just days ago. Free agency patched holes, yet this roster, still raw from January’s fade, craves that defensive alpha to flank a young core itching for contention. Peters’ poker face hints at preparedness for a slide-back deal, but don’t bet on it; last year’s Josh Conerly grab at 29 proved they pounce on value. Watch No. 7 like a hawk April 23—Reese or Styles could redefine 2026’s ceiling, thrusting Washington toward NFC East menace.
